# Test fixture B — copula avoidance + signposting (technical-nonfiction)

The local-first model serves as a counterweight to the assumption that
infrastructure must be rented. Each device represents a fully-functioning
node in the user's data graph, and the network stands as an optimization
layer rather than a precondition.

Let's dive into what this means in practice. Here's what you need to know:
the user's machine is the authoritative copy. The cloud serves as a sync
relay, not a master record. This marks a pivotal moment in how we think
about distributed storage. It underscores the broader trend toward
user-controlled data ownership and contributes to an evolving landscape
of sovereignty-aware architecture.

The architecture features three components. The kernel serves as the
trust boundary. The daemon represents the sync surface. The relay
embodies the network optimization. Together, these constitute a complete
local-first runtime.

In reality, the most important question is whether the developer
experience can match the SaaS baseline. At its core, what really matters
is the migration cost for teams already locked into hosted platforms.
Now let's look at the migration paths.

The migration paths can be categorized into three approaches: incremental
adoption, parallel deployment, and ground-up rewriting. Each approach
showcases different tradeoffs. Each approach features different cost
profiles. Each approach represents a different commitment level.
